english: 1. doctrine, a particular doctrine, tenet, or precept; gsaṅ-bai čos śig (gsang bai chos shig) an esoteric doctrine, a mystery Dzl.; ǩyád-čos (khyad chos) for kyad-par-čan-gyi čos (khyad par can gyi chos) a peculiar, distinguished, sublime, and therefore difficult doctrine; jig-rtén-gyi čos brgyad ('jig rten gyi chos brgyad) the eight doctrines or principles of the world (though frq. mentioned, I found them nowhere specified) cf. Foucaux Gyatcherr., Translation p. 264; čos-brgyád-mǩan (chos brgyad mkhan), a man of the world, worldling Mil. — More esp. 2. moral doctrine, whether any separate dogma, or the sum of various doctrines, religion in general, both theoretically (system of morality, ethics) and practically (faith, exercise of religion); lha-čos (lha chos) the religion of the gods or (Buddhist) deities, i.e. the Buddhist religion, as the only true one, in opposition to all other heresies and false religions (log-čos (log chos)), as well as to irreligiousness (čos ma yin-pa (chos ma yin pa)); ǩa-čos (kha chos) profession with the lips, hypocrisy Glr.; °krig-pai čos-la brtén-pa ('khrig pai chos la brten pa) those practising the religion of voluptuousness (an expression designedly forcible, like St. Paul's: 'whose god is their belly'); mí-čos (mi chos) v. below; čos °čád-pa (chos 'chad pa), or bšád-pa (bshad pa), stón-pa (ston pa), smrá-ba (smra ba), sgróg-pa (sgrog pa), resp. čos-kyi sgrog-gléṅ mdzád-pa (chos kyi sgrog gleng mdzad pa) Glr. to expound, to teach, to preach religion; čos smrá-bai žál-la ltá-ba (chos smra bai zhal la lta ba) to watch the mouth of the preacher Pth.; *čo dóg-pa* (cho dog pa) C. to read a religious book; *čos šád-ǩan* (chos bshad mkhan) W. a preacher; čos °čád-pai °dun-ǩaṅ (chos 'chad pai 'dun khang) place where sermons are delivered, church Dzl.; čos nyán-pa (chos nyan pa) to hear religious discourses Dzl.; čos žú-ba (chos zhu ba) to ask for religious discourses; partic.: one eagerly desiring or asking for religious instruction, an inquirer Pth., Mil.; čos byéd-pa (chos byed pa) to act or live religiously, righteously, = čos bžín-du byéd-pa (chos bzhin du byed pa); also merely to wish to become pious, to strive after piety; kyed snyiṅ-nas čos byéd-na (khyed snying nas chos byed na) if you are in good earnest about religion, if piety is the aim of your heart Mil.; lastly in a special sense: to become or to be a monk Pth.; čós-la sems sgyúr-ba (chos la sems sgyur ba) Mil. to show an inclination for religion, to turn religious; čós-su (chos su), or čós-la °júg-pa (chos la 'jug pa) 1. vb. nt. to enter into religion, to be converted, also: to go over to a religion, to turn (Buddhist), 2. vb. act. to convert, to turn a person from a bad life to a good one, to make him a believer, to make another a convert, a proselyte; čós-la °gód-pa (chos la 'god pa) = °júg-pa ('jug pa) 1; čos spyód-pa (chos spyod pa) to practise religion; the exercise of religion, worship S.g.; bka-čós (bka' chos) the word of Buddha, the doctrine as taught by Buddha himself; rtógs-pai čos (rtogs pai chos) Thgy. the knowledge acquired by meditation, independently of books, scarcely different from ṅes-dón (nges don), or ṅon-šés (mngon shes); bstán-pai čos (bstan pai chos) Thgy. any knowledge derived from other sources. — 3. in a special sense the religion of Buddha, Buddhism, dám-pai čos (dam pai chos), and frq. dám-pa čos (dam pa chos) id. (cf. ἡ πίστις Acts 6, 7); čos daṅ bon (chos dang bon) Buddhism and Bon-religion Mil.; čós-la lóṅ-spyod-par °gyúr-ba (chos la long spyod par 'gyur ba) to live in the enjoyment of true faith. — 4. religious writings, and writings, books, literature in general, in as much as the Tibetans derivate every science from religion; bón-gyi čos tams-čád (bon gyi chos thams cad) all the Bon-writings Mil. — 5. custom, manner, common usage, fashion, mí-čos (mi chos) manners of the world Mil.; mí-čos-kyi dús-su (mi chos kyi dus su) as long as he lived according to the ways of the world Mil.; yúl-čos-kyis (yul chos kyis) according to the custom of the country Dzl.; ǩyád-čos (khyad chos) the way of distinguishing, of pointing out the characteristics Glr. (cf. under 1); nature, quality, Dzl. 74, 18 cf. čos-nyíd (chos nyid). — 6. substance, being, thing, čos tams-čád mi-rtág-pa yin-gyi (chos thams cad mi rtag pa yin gyi) as every thing existing is perishable Dzl. — Other philosophical expressions containing the word čos (chos) v. Was. (296). Comp. *čos-skad* (chos skad) W. book-language, as opp. to p̌ál-skad (phal skad). — čós-sku (chos sku) v. sku (sku). — čos-skyóṅ (chos skyong) v. skyoṅ-ba (skyong ba). — čos-kri (chos khri) reading-desk, lecturer's chair, pulpit Pth.; reading-table, school-desk. — čos-kríms (chos khrims) v. krims (khrims). — čos-krims-pa (chos khrims pa) v. dge-bskós (dge bskos). — čos-kór (chos 'khor) vulgo prayer-mill; the column of disks on the mčod-rtén (mchod rten) Pth. v. °kór-lo ('khor lo) 2, also °kór-lo ('khor lo) extr. — čos-grá (chos grwa) school. — čos-(kyi) rgyál(-po) (chos kyi rgyal po) 1. honorary title of kings deserving
